title: Exception Handling
beginnerExplanation: Exception handling with try/except lets your program gracefully handle errors instead of crashing. It's how you build resilient, production-ready code.
intermediateExplanation: Real programs encounter unexpected situations: files that don't exist, network failures, invalid user input. Exception handling lets you anticipate and recover from these problems.

realWorldAnalogy.title: A Safety Net
realWorldAnalogy.description: A trapeze artist performs risky moves (the try block). If they fall, the safety net catches them (the except block). Without it, a single mistake is catastrophic.

mentalModel: A trapeze artist performs risky moves (the try block). If they fall, the safety net catches them (the except block). Without it, a single mistake is catastrophic.
visualDiagram:
graph TD
  Start --> Process[Exception Handling]
  Process --> End
executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Using bare except: which catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt.
Silencing errors with an empty except block (pass) — at least log them.
Putting too much code in the try block — keep it minimal.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Exception Handling but hit a common pitfall: Using bare except: which catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt.
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Catch specific exceptions (except ValueError) instead of bare except.

practiceQuestions.question: What does the `finally` block do?
options: Runs only if an exception occurs
Runs only if no exception occurs
Always runs, whether or not an exception occurred
practiceQuestions.explanation: The finally block executes no matter what — even if an exception was raised, caught, or not caught. It's ideal for cleanup tasks.

challengeQuestions.question: What is the correct keyword or operator most commonly associated with Exception Handling?
hints: Look at the syntax summary of the topic.
Check standard keyword usage in Python.

projectExercise.title: Mastering Exception Handling
projectExercise.description: Implement a clean snippet demonstrating the use of Exception Handling.
requirements:
Write a snippet utilizing the syntax: try:
    # risky code
except ErrorType as e:
    # handle error
finally:
    # always runs

bossBattle.mechanic: Resolve syntax bugs and refactor code structure.

I can avoid Using bare except: which catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt..
confusionPoints: Using bare except: which catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt.
Silencing errors with an empty except block (pass) — at least log them.
Putting too much code in the try block — keep it minimal.
simplifiedExplanation: Exception handling with try/except lets your program gracefully handle errors instead of crashing. It's how you build resilient, production-ready code.

retentionSystem.revision1: Define the core syntax of Exception Handling.
retentionSystem.revision2: Name one common mistake when using Exception Handling.

developerMode.industryUsage: Exception Handling is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Catch specific exceptions (except ValueError) instead of bare except.
Use finally for cleanup code that must always run (closing files, connections).
Don't use exceptions for normal control flow — they're for exceptional situations.
antiPatterns: Using bare except: which catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt.
Silencing errors with an empty except block (pass) — at least log them.
Putting too much code in the try block — keep it minimal.
developerMode.architectureNotes: Interpreted and executed in the primary application call loop.
